name: Vaughn
short_description: BLM public land near Vaughn offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Flat terrain makes this an accessible spot for beginners and families.
description:
Vaughn sits on productive Bureau of Land Management property in central Montana's Cascade County, where you can collect semi-precious stones without permits or fees. This location is part of the Missouri River drainage basin, an area known for quality agate and jasper deposits left behind by ancient volcanic and sedimentary processes. The terrain here is characteristically flat and open, making it ideal for first-time rockhounders and families who want to search without technical climbing or long hikes.

The landscape around Vaughn reflects Montana's transitional zone between the Rocky Mountains and the Great Plains. Erosion and weathering have exposed colorful chalcedony and agate nodules in the surrounding washes and gravels, many showing the banding and color patterns typical of this region. You will find specimens ranging from small thumb-sized pieces to larger chunks suitable for lapidary work.

Visitors should expect to spend time walking across open ground and scanning surface material in draws and creek beds. The flat slope means minimal elevation gain, and most productive areas are within easy walking distance of pullouts. Early morning or late afternoon visits often yield better light for spotting translucent specimens.
history: This area sits within the Northern Great Plains geological province, where Cretaceous sedimentary formations and Tertiary volcanic deposits created conditions favorable for silica-rich mineral precipitation. Agate and jasper nodules formed within ancient hydrothermal systems and were later exposed through erosion along the Missouri River drainage, a process that continues to expose new material today.
tips: Search dry washes and eroded banks where water and weather have concentrated heavier stones and exposed fresh nodule surfaces. Look for pieces with translucent edges or waxy luster that indicate chalcedony or agate rather than ordinary quartz. A small hand lens helps you spot banding and color variation in specimens before collecting. Bring a bucket or backpack to stay within the 25 lb daily limit, and focus on areas where gravels have been sorted by water rather than solid bedrock outcrops.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3,369 feet with flat to gently rolling terrain. Open grassland and sagebrush with scattered gravels and small washes. Walking is easy across most surfaces with sturdy shoes sufficient; no steep grades or technical footing required. Parking areas are informal pullouts along access roads, typically less than a half-mile walk to productive collecting zones.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall offers stable weather and dry ground conditions that make walking and material exposure optimal. Winter snow and mud make access difficult; summer heat is moderate at this elevation.
written_directions: From Vaughn, travel north or east toward BLM-managed lands in the Cascade County area. The specific access points vary depending on which section of BLM land you target. Contact the Bureau of Land Management Great Falls Field Office for current road conditions and recommended entry points, as some roads may be closed seasonally or require high-clearance vehicles after rain. GPS navigation to the coordinates 47.569086, -111.551132 will place you near prime rockhounding zones; look for informal pullouts where other rockhounders have parked.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt and gravel roads. Passenger vehicles suitable in dry conditions; avoid after heavy rain. Some sections may require high-clearance vehicles depending on recent maintenance.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along access roads. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space for 2-4 vehicles typical at each pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Surface collection only; do not dig into bedrock. Respect any posted seasonal closures or habitat protection areas.
rules:

No motorized excavation

nearest_services: Vaughn has limited services. Cascade, approximately 15 miles south, offers gas and food. Great Falls, 35 miles south, has full retail and hospital services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most fitness levels. Unpaved roads to parking areas but easy walking once parked. No wheelchair-accessible facilities, but lack of steep grades makes it suitable for families with young children and those with mobility considerations. Sturdy walking shoes recommended.

faq.question: What types of rocks and gems can you find rockhounding near Vaughn, Montana?
faq.answer: The Vaughn area on BLM land is known for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Most finds are semi-precious nodules with attractive banding and color, ranging from small pieces to specimens weighing several pounds. The agate here often displays cream, tan, and brown tones typical of the Northern Great Plains geological province.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Vaughn BLM land in Cascade County?
faq.answer: No, rockhounding at Vaughn is completely free. This is public Bureau of Land Management land open to recreational collecting at no cost. You do not need a permit, but you must follow the 25 lb daily limit and personal use rules established by the FLPMA.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Vaughn, Montana?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal, when weather is stable and ground conditions are dry. Late spring through early fall provides the best combination of warm temperatures and accessible terrain. Winter brings snow and mud that make access difficult; summer heat at this elevation remains moderate and manageable.

faq.question: Is Vaughn rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Vaughn is an excellent beginner and family-friendly location. The flat terrain requires no hiking skills or special equipment, and kids as young as 6 can participate in surface collecting. No permit or technical knowledge is required; you simply walk the open ground, scan for colorful specimens, and collect what you find up to 25 lbs per day.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Vaughn?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy walking shoes, a bucket or backpack to carry specimens within the 25 lb limit, a hand lens to inspect pieces, and sun protection. A small chisel or pry bar can help you free specimens from surrounding material, though surface collection is primary here. Bring plenty of water, as the open terrain offers limited shade.

faq.question: How far is Vaughn rockhounding from the nearest town with gas and food?
faq.answer: Cascade is approximately 15 miles from Vaughn, offering basic gas and food services. Great Falls, about 35 miles away, has full retail amenities and hospitals. Cell service is spotty in the Vaughn area, so plan your route and time accordingly.

meta_title: Vaughn Rockhounding - Agate & Jasper in Montana
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Vaughn, Montana. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony on public land. No permit required. Family-friendly collecting.
